---
title: "Preprocessing: obtaining census spatial files and calculating travel times"
author: "Bruno Santos & Antonio Paez"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  tufte::tufte_handout:
    citation_package: natbib
    latex_engine: xelatex
  tufte::tufte_html: default
  tufte::tufte_book:
    citation_package: natbib
    latex_engine: xelatex
bibliography: skeleton.bib
link-citations: yes
editor_options: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

```{r clean-workspace, include=FALSE}
# cleaning objects from the workspace 
rm(list = ls())
```

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
# layout configuration 
library(tufte)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(warning = FALSE, message = FALSE)
options(htmltools.dir.version = FALSE)
```

```{r}
#library('CommuteCA') # Access data sets from CommuteCA R package
library('cancensus') # Access census spatial files
library('geojsonsf') # GeoJSON to Simple Feature Converter
library('data.table') # fast aggregation of large data 
library('dplyr') # a grammar of Data Manipulation
library('here') # enable easy file referencing in project-oriented workflows
library('rJava') # allows creation of objects, calling methods and accessing fields.
library('r5r') # rapid realistic routing with R5 in R
library('sf') # support for simple features, a standardized way to encode spatial vector data
```

# Introduction

This file is an adaptation of an Rmarkdown from the  [**CommuteCA**](https://github.com/dias-bruno/CommuteCA) package. CommuteCA was created in conjunction with the office of the [*Research
Data Center* at *McMaster University*](https://rdc.mcmaster.ca/), the
[*Sherman Centre for Digital Scholarship*](https://scds.ca/) and the
[*Mobilizing Justice*](https://mobilizingjustice.ca/)[^1].

[^1]: The Mobilizing Justice project is a multidisciplinary and
    multi-sector collaboration with the objective of understand and
    address transportation poverty in Canada and to improve the
    well-being of Canadians at risk of transport poverty. The Social
    Sciences and Humanities Research Council (SSRHC) has provided
    funding for the project, which was created by an unprecedented
    alliance of academics from various Canadian provinces and
    institutions, transportation firms, and nonprofit organizations

The main objective of the *CommuteCA* package is to develop standardized
methods for transport analysis in research, especially for studies using
Statistics Canada surveys. Among the available surveys, we focused our
efforts on the [*2021 Census of
Population*](https://www12.statcan.gc.ca/census-recensement/index-eng.cfm),
which contain valuable variables for transportation research.

In this Rmarkdown, we will perform the preprocessing stages to identify the effect of calculated accessibility on perceived accessibility. More specifically, we'll demonstrate how to obtain the census spatial data files from the [`cancensus`](https://mountainmath.github.io/cancensus/) library; and how to calculate a travel time matrix for multimodal transport networks (walk, bike, public transport and car-motorized), for an selected area using the [{r5r}](https://ipeagit.github.io/r5r/) R package [@Pereira2021]. 

## Suggested Readings

-   Pereira, R. H. M., Saraiva, M., Herszenhut, D., Braga, C. K. V., & Conway, M. W. (2021). r5r: Rapid Realistic Routing on Multimodal Transport Networks with R5 in R. Findings, 21262. https://doi.org/10.32866/001c.21262.

# Creating folders to save the spatial files 

First, we will create a folder within the `raw-data` directory
using the code of our study area. This organization makes it easier
to manage files if we decide to analyze multiple study areas.

```{r}
code_label <- "GTAH"
directory <- paste0(here(), "/data-raw/output/", code_label,"/spatial-files/")

if(!dir.exists(directory)){
  dir.create(directory, recursive = TRUE)}
```

# Obtaining data files using the `cancensus` library

To perform the next steps of our accessibility analysis, we need to
download/obtain the census spatial files. More specifically, we will download the following data sets:

-   [Census
    Divisions](https://www12.statcan.gc.ca/census-recensement/2021/ref/dict/az/Definition-eng.cfm?ID=geo008):
    Census division (CD) is the general term for provincially legislated
    areas (such as county, municipalité régionale de comté (MRC) and
    regional district) or their equivalents in Canada. Census divisions
    are intermediate geographic areas between the province/territory
    level and the municipality (census subdivision).
-   [Census
    Subdivisions](https://www12.statcan.gc.ca/census-recensement/2021/ref/dict/az/Definition-eng.cfm?ID=geo012): Census subdivision (CSD) is the general term for municipalities or areas treated as municipal equivalents for statistical purposes.
-   [Dissemination
    areas](https://www12.statcan.gc.ca/census-recensement/2021/ref/dict/az/definition-eng.cfm?ID=geo021):
    A dissemination area (DA) is a small, relatively stable geographic
    unit composed of one or more adjacent dissemination blocks with an
    average population of 400 to 700 persons based on data from the
    previous Census of Population Program. It is the smallest standard
    geographic area for which all census data are disseminated. *Only
    for analysis at the Dissemination area's level.*
-   [Census
    tracts](https://www12.statcan.gc.ca/census-recensement/2021/ref/dict/az/Definition-eng.cfm?ID=geo013):
    Census tracts (CTs) are small, relatively stable geographic areas
    that usually have a population of fewer than 7,500 persons, based on
    data from the previous Census of Population Program. They are
    located in census metropolitan areas (CMAs) and in census
    agglomerations (CAs) that had a core population of 50,000 or more in
    the previous census.

We will prepare our data set using the
[`cancensus`](https://mountainmath.github.io/cancensus/) library, but
you can also access the data directly from the Census website, by
clicking in this
[link](https://www12.statcan.gc.ca/census-recensement/2021/geo/sip-pis/boundary-limites/index2021-eng.cfm?year=21).

First, you need install the `cancensus` library [@cancensus]., `cancensus` requires a valid `CensusMapper` API key to use. You can
obtain a free API key by [signing
up](https://censusmapper.ca/users/sign_up) for a CensusMapper account.
To check your API key, just go to “Edit Profile” (in the top-right of
the CensusMapper menu bar). Once you have your key, you can store it in
your system environment so it is automatically used in API calls. To do
so just enter `set_cancensus_api_key('<your_api_key>', install = TRUE)`.

```{r}
set_cancensus_api_key('CensusMapper_67b343a1715082af0a9f926dfc6390e8', install = TRUE, overwrite=TRUE)
```

You can visualize all available data set in the library by doing:

```{r}
list_census_datasets()
```

For this demonstration, we will access the data set related to the City
of Toronto (Census Divisions (CD) = `3520`).

```{r}
# As a character! you can change for your city code
#code <- c("3525") 

code <- c("3518", # Durham
          "3519", # York
          "3520", # Toronto
          "3521", # Peel
          "3524", # Halton
          "3525" # Hamilton
          ) 
```

In our case, we are interested on the Census Divisions:

```{r}
census_divisions <- get_census(dataset='CA21', 
                               regions=list(CD=code), 
                               level='CD', 
                               geo_format = "sf")
```

Filtering and adjusting variable names:

```{r}
census_divisions <- census_divisions %>% 
  dplyr::select(GeoUID, name, PR_UID, Population, `Shape Area`, geometry) %>% 
  dplyr::rename(CDUID = GeoUID, 
                CDPOPULATION = Population,
                CDNAME = name,
                PRUID = PR_UID)
```

```{r}
plot(census_divisions["geometry"])
```

Census subdivisions:  

```{r}
census_subdivisions <- get_census(dataset='CA21', 
                               regions=list(CD=code),
                               level='CSD', 
                               geo_format = "sf")
```

Filtering and adjusting variable names:

```{r}
census_subdivisions <- census_subdivisions %>% 
  filter(!is.na(CMA_UID)) %>% # Selecting only the CSD within metropolitan areas
  dplyr::select(GeoUID, name, CD_UID, PR_UID, Population, `Shape Area`, geometry) %>% 
  dplyr::rename(CSDUID = GeoUID, 
                CSDNAME = name,
                CSDPOPULATION = Population,
                PRUID = PR_UID, 
                CDUID = CD_UID) 

census_subdivisions <- census_subdivisions %>% 
  left_join(census_divisions %>% st_drop_geometry() %>% 
              dplyr::select(CDUID, CDNAME), by = "CDUID" )
```

```{r}
plot(census_subdivisions["geometry"])
```
Census tracts (it is important to remember that if you city of analysis
is not within a Census Agglomeration or Census Metropolitan Area, you
will not have census tract available for your city!):

```{r}
census_tracts <- get_census(dataset='CA21', regions=list(CD=code), level='CT', geo_format = "sf")
```

Filtering and adjusting variable names:

```{r}
census_tracts <- census_tracts %>% 
  dplyr::select(GeoUID, name, `Shape Area`, Population, Households, CD_UID, CMA_UID, CSD_UID, geometry) %>% 
  dplyr::rename(CTUID = GeoUID, 
                CTNAME = name,
                PCD = CD_UID,
                LANDAREA =`Shape Area`,
                CMAUID = CMA_UID,
                PCSD = CSD_UID) %>% 
  mutate(PRUID = substr(CMAUID, start = 1, stop = 2),
         CMAUID = substr(CMAUID, start = 3, stop = 5)) %>% 
  dplyr::select(CTUID,CTNAME, LANDAREA, PCD, PCSD, CMAUID, PRUID, Population, Households, geometry) %>% 
  left_join(census_subdivisions %>% 
              dplyr::select(CSDUID, CSDNAME, CDNAME) %>% 
              st_drop_geometry(), by = c("PCSD" = "CSDUID"))
```

```{r}
plot(census_tracts["geometry"])
```

And Dissemination areas:

```{r}
dissemination_areas <- get_census(dataset='CA21', regions=list(CD=code), level='DA', geo_format = "sf")
```

Filtering and adjusting variable names:

```{r}
dissemination_areas <- dissemination_areas %>% 
  dplyr::rename(DAUID = GeoUID, 
                PCD = CD_UID,
                LANDAREA =`Shape Area`,
                CMAUID = CMA_UID,
                CTUID = CT_UID) %>% 
  mutate(PRUID = substr(CMAUID, start = 1, stop = 2),
         CMAUID = substr(CMAUID, start = 3, stop = 5)) %>% 
  dplyr::select(DAUID, LANDAREA, PCD, CTUID, CSD_UID, CMAUID, PRUID, Population, geometry) %>% 
  left_join(census_subdivisions %>% 
              dplyr::select(CSDUID, CSDNAME, CDNAME) %>% 
              st_drop_geometry(), by = c("CSD_UID" = "CSDUID"))
```

```{r}
plot(dissemination_areas["geometry"])
```

## Download from the Census Website

You can also download the files from the 2021 Census Website.

As an example, we will download the Population Centres file that will also be used in future analysis.

1. First, you need to go to: https://www12.statcan.gc.ca/census-recensement/2021/geo/sip-pis/boundary-limites/index2021-eng.cfm?year=21
2. Set as parameters: Language: English; Type: Cartographic Boundary Files (CBF); Statistical boundaries: Population centres; Format: Shapefile (.shp). 
3. Continue to next page and download the zip file.
4. Extract the zip file. 

And then:

```{r}
pop_centre_address <- "C:/Users/brdia/Downloads/lpc_000b21a_e/lpc_000b21a_e.shp"  # Update to your machine

population_centres <- st_read(pop_centre_address)

population_centres <- population_centres %>% 
  mutate(pc_class = case_when(PCCLASS == "2" ~ "Small", 
                               PCCLASS == "3" ~ "Medium", 
                               PCCLASS == "4" ~ "Large", 
                               TRUE ~ "Rural"), 
         pc_type = case_when(PCTYPE == "1" ~ "Core", 
                               PCTYPE == "2" ~ "Fringe", 
                               PCTYPE == "3" ~ "Secondary core", 
                               TRUE ~ "Rural")) %>% 
  sf::st_transform(st_crs(census_subdivisions)) %>% 
  st_make_valid()
```

Extract only the population centres within the study area: 

```{r}
population_centres <- population_centres %>% 
  sf::st_crop(sf::st_boundary(census_subdivisions))

plot(population_centres['geometry'])
```

# Travel Time Matrix

To create the travel time matrix, we'll need the following data files:

- The spatial unit used as a reference to aggregate Census data and used as places of origin and destination, which can be dissemination areas or census tracts. 
-   A road network data set from OpenStreetMap in `.pbf` format. We obtained the road network for Ontario from the [OpenStreetMap  Geofabrik](https://download.geofabrik.de/north-america/canada/ontario.html).
-   Public transport feeds in General Transit Feed
Specification (GTFS) format, obtained from the [Canadian Public Transit Network
Database](https://www150.statcan.gc.ca/n1/pub/23-26-0003/232600032025001-eng.htm)
-   A raster file of Digital Elevation Model data in .tif (optional). We obtained the `.tif` files from [Canadian Digital Surface Model Project](https://open.canada.ca/data/en/dataset/768570f8-5761-498a-bd6a-315eb6cc023d), which provide elevation raster files with around 20 meters of spatial resolution for all Canada.

The {r5r} requires the [Java SE Development Kit 21](https://www.oracle.com/java/technologies/downloads/#java21). Also, it's necessary to set the environmental variable to use Java^[Check: https://confluence.atlassian.com/doc/setting-the-java_home-variable-in-windows-8895.html . Additionally, you may have to set the maximum memory allocation for Java, in this case: run the following code your R console windows: *options(java.parameters = "-Xmx12g")*. 

The number *12* in the code above indicates the total size of RAM you're allocating for java (12 gigabytes). More RAM indicates more fast processing, but it'll depends on your computer operational system. You can allocate more or less memory to Java changing the number *12* for other number, as "-Xmx4g" for 4 gigabytes, or "-Xmx10g" for 10 gigabytes. 

| ⚠️**NOTE:**  allocate memory before loading {r5r}. The memory needs to be consistent with the system where this is run. In BS's laptop this is set to 10 Gigabytes
```{r java-ram}
# Allocate memory for routing
options(java.parameters = "-Xmx30G")
```

First, define what will be your spatial unit to calculate the travel time: census tract or dissemination areas. In our analysis, we will use the CT level to perform our analsyis: 

```{r}
# For census tract
spatial_unit <- census_tracts
spatial_unit_type <- 'CT'

# For dissemination areas
# spatial_unit <- dissemination_areas
# spatial_unit_type <- 'DA' 
```

Place the GTFS files and the optional raster files `.tif` in the `directory_r5r`:

For the travel time matrix, we will create the following folder:

```{r r5r-core}
directory_r5r <- paste0(here(), "/data-raw/output/" , code_label, "/", spatial_unit_type , "/travel_times/")

if(!dir.exists(directory_r5r)){
  dir.create(directory_r5r, recursive = TRUE)}
```

```{r}
directory_r5r
```

For the road network file, we will first reduce it to include only the roads within the study area. To do this, we need to download the [Osmosis](https://wiki.openstreetmap.org/wiki/Osmosis/Installation), a command line Java application for processing OSM data.

After downloading Osmosis, we need to create a variable that refers to its path: 
```{r}
osmosis_path <- "C:/Users/brdia/Downloads/osmosis-0.49.2/osmosis-0.49.2/bin/osmosis.bat"
```

Then, inform where is located the large road network file:
```{r}
large_pbf_path <- "C:/Users/brdia/Downloads/ontario-251221.osm.pbf"
```

and then run the following chunk to reduce your road network to select only roads within your study area: 

```{r}
area_bbox <- sf::st_bbox(spatial_unit)

smaller_pbf <- paste0(directory_r5r, "study_area.pbf")

# Prepare call to osmosis
osmosis_cmd <- sprintf("%s --read-pbf %s --bounding-box left=%s bottom=%s right=%s top=%s --write-pbf %s",
                       osmosis_path, large_pbf_path, 
                       area_bbox$xmin, area_bbox$ymin, area_bbox$xmax, area_bbox$ymax,
                       smaller_pbf)

# Call to osmosis
shell(osmosis_cmd, translate = TRUE)
```

Then, you can create the r5rcore: 

```{r r5r-core}
r5r_core <- r5r::build_network(data_path = directory_r5r, 
                               verbose = TRUE, 
                               overwrite = FALSE)
```

We will calculate the travel times between each polygon of the spatial unit selected for the analysis, considering their centroid as origins and destinations. Converting the spatial units to simple features and taking the centroids from each polygon:

```{r centroid}
spatial_unit_centroids <-  data.frame(id = as.data.frame(st_centroid(spatial_unit))[,1], 
                           st_centroid(spatial_unit) %>%
                             st_transform(crs = 4326) %>%
                             st_coordinates())

colnames(spatial_unit_centroids) <- c("id", "lon", "lat")
```

We'll use the `travel_time_matrix()` function from the {r5r} `R` package to estimate the travel time for each origin-destination pair on a set departure date. The function uses some travel parameters that can be viewed on the [package's documents page](https://ipeagit.github.io/r5r/), but in our case we'll use the following parameters and inputs:

- Origin: the centroids of the selected spatial unit.
- Destination: the centroids of the selected spatial unit.
- Mode of transport: walking, cycling, public transport and car.
- A departure date.
- Maximum travel time: maximum value of travel time identified for the transportation mode. The {max_travel_times_pcd.rda} and {max_travel_times_cma.rda} tables contain the maximum travel time for each census division and census metropolitan area, respectively, taken from the 2021 Census.^[You can verify the maximum travel time for your census division or census metropolitan area by running: _data(max_travel_times_pcd)_ or _data(max_travel_times_cma)_ . To verify the PDC or CMA code of your study area, see: https://www12.statcan.gc.ca/census-recensement/2021/ref/dict/az/Definition-eng.cfm?ID=geo027 .]
- Walking time: The maximum walking time (in minutes) to access and exit the transit network, to make transfers within the network, or to complete walking-only trips.

Setting the departure time:
 
```{r departure-time}
departure_datetime <- as.POSIXct("22-12-2025 8:00:00", format = "%d-%m-%Y %H:%M:%S")
```

## Travel time matrix for car/motor mode

Considering the car/motor mode, the maximum trip duration for the study area, according to the Census, is: 240 minutes. However, we due to the size of study area, we will set a maximum travel time `180` of for this mode.

```{r duration-car}
max_trip_duration_car <- 180
```

Generating the travel time:

```{r ttm-car}
ttm_car <- travel_time_matrix(r5r_core = r5r_core,
                               origins = spatial_unit_centroids,
                               destinations = spatial_unit_centroids,
                               mode = "CAR",
                               departure_datetime = departure_datetime,
                               max_trip_duration = max_trip_duration_car,
                               max_walk_time = 5,
                               time_window = 3)

ttm_car <- ttm_car %>%
  
  mutate(mode = "Car-motor")

write.csv(ttm_car, paste0(directory_r5r, "ttm_car.csv"), row.names=FALSE)
```


## Travel time matrix for walking mode

Considering the walking mode, the maximum trip duration for the study area, according to the Census, is: 240 minutes. However, we due to the size of study area, we will set a maximum travel time `90` of for this mode.

```{r duration-wak}
max_trip_duration_walk <- 90
```

Generating the travel time:

```{r ttm-wak}
ttm_walk <- travel_time_matrix(r5r_network = r5r_core,
                          origins = spatial_unit_centroids,
                          destinations = spatial_unit_centroids,
                          mode = "WALK",
                          departure_datetime = departure_datetime,
                          max_trip_duration = max_trip_duration_walk)

ttm_walk <- ttm_walk %>%
            mutate(mode = "Walk")

write.csv(ttm_walk, paste0(directory_r5r, "ttm_walk.csv"), row.names=FALSE)
```

## Travel time matrix for transit mode

Considering the transit mode, the maximum trip duration for the study area, according to the Census, is: 240 minutes. However, we due to the size of study area, we will set a maximum travel time `180` of for this mode.

```{r duration-transit}
max_trip_duration_transit <- 180
```

Generating the travel time:

```{r ttm-transit}
ttm_transit <- travel_time_matrix(r5r_network = r5r_core,
                               origins = spatial_unit_centroids,
                               destinations = spatial_unit_centroids,
                               mode = "TRANSIT",
                               departure_datetime = departure_datetime,
                               max_trip_duration = max_trip_duration_transit,
                               max_walk_time = 15, 
                               max_rides = 3)

ttm_transit <- ttm_transit %>%
  mutate(mode = "Transit")

write.csv(ttm_transit, paste0(directory_r5r, "ttm_transit.csv"), row.names=FALSE)
```

## Travel time matrix for cycling mode

Considering the cycling mode, the maximum trip duration for the study area, according to the Census, is: 240 minutes.  However, we due to the size of study area, we will set a maximum travel time `240` of for this mode.

```{r duration-cycling}
max_trip_duration_bike <- 90
```

Generating the travel time:

```{r ttm-cycling}
ttm_bike <- travel_time_matrix(r5r_network = r5r_core,
                               origins = spatial_unit_centroids,
                               destinations = spatial_unit_centroids,
                               mode = "BICYCLE",
                               departure_datetime = departure_datetime,
                               max_trip_duration = max_trip_duration_bike,
                               max_walk_time = 5, 
                               max_lts = 3)

ttm_bike <- ttm_bike %>%
  mutate(mode = "Bike")

write.csv(ttm_bike, paste0(directory_r5r, "ttm_bike.csv"), row.names=FALSE)
```

## Travel time matrix for all modes

Combine the travel times for each mode in a unique data frame:

```{r}
# ttm_bike <- read.csv(paste0(directory_r5r, "ttm_bike.csv"),
#                colClasses = c("character","character", "numeric", "character"))
# ttm_walk <- read.csv(paste0(directory_r5r, "ttm_walk.csv"),
#                colClasses = c("character","character", "numeric", "character"))
# ttm_transit <- read.csv(paste0(directory_r5r, "ttm_transit.csv"),
#                colClasses = c("character","character", "numeric", "character"))
# ttm_car <- read.csv(paste0(directory_r5r, "ttm_car.csv"),
#                colClasses = c("character","character", "numeric", "character"))
```

```{r ttm}
ttm <- rbind(ttm_bike,
             ttm_car,
             ttm_transit,
             ttm_walk) %>%
  mutate(mode = factor(mode, levels = c("Bike","Car-motor","Transit","Walk"))) %>%
  rename(travel_time = travel_time_p50,
         PwMode_label = mode)
```

Bring the Census Metropolitan Area information:

```{r ttm-cma}
names(spatial_unit)[1] <- 'SPUID'

ttm_spatial_unit <- ttm %>%
  mutate(from_id = as.factor(from_id),
         to_id = as.factor(to_id),
         PwMode_label = as.factor(PwMode_label)) %>%
  left_join(spatial_unit %>% 
            dplyr::select("SPUID","PCSD","CSDNAME", "PCD","CDNAME", "CMAUID", "PRUID") %>% 
              st_drop_geometry(), 
            by= c("from_id"= "SPUID"))
```

# Data export

To finalize, we will export the data:

```{r}
sf::st_write(population_centres, paste0(directory, "population_centres.gpkg"), append=FALSE)

sf::st_write(census_divisions, paste0(directory, "census_divisions.gpkg"), append=FALSE)

sf::st_write(census_subdivisions, paste0(directory, "census_subdivisions.gpkg"), append=FALSE)

sf::st_write(census_tracts, paste0(directory, "census_tracts.gpkg"), append=FALSE)

sf::st_write(dissemination_areas, paste0(directory, "dissemination_areas.gpkg"), append=FALSE)
```

```{r export-ttm}
write.csv(ttm_spatial_unit, paste0(directory_r5r, "ttm.csv"), row.names=FALSE)
```

# References {#references .unnumbered}
